name: Tragedy
description: A protagonist's flaw or irreversible choice leads to downfall, emphasizing inevitability and moral consequence.

nodes.label: Elevated State
nodes.definition: The protagonist begins in a position of strength, virtue, or promise. They have something real to lose — status, love, integrity, or potential.
entry_conditions: The audience sees the character's admirable qualities and their position
exit_conditions: A temptation or pressure activates the character's latent flaw
typical_variants: Loving person in a stable relationship
failure_modes: Character starts too low — there's no height from which to fall
Elevated state is unearned, making the audience indifferent to loss
signals_in_text: A hint of the flaw beneath the surface

nodes.label: Temptation or Pressure
nodes.definition: A situation arises that activates the protagonist's specific flaw — ambition, jealousy, pride, desire, or fear. The flaw was always present but now has an object.
entry_conditions: An external circumstance creates the opportunity for the flaw to act
exit_conditions: The protagonist makes a choice that begins the descent
typical_variants: Offer of power that requires moral compromise
Fear of loss that drives desperate action
failure_modes: Temptation is too mild to justify what follows
The flaw is not established before the temptation arrives

nodes.label: Fatal Choice
nodes.definition: The protagonist makes the irreversible decision that sets the tragedy in motion. They cross a moral or practical line that cannot be uncrossed.
entry_conditions: The protagonist could still turn back but chooses not to
exit_conditions: The action is taken; consequences are now inevitable
typical_variants: Abandoning a moral principle for advantage
failure_modes: Choice is forced rather than chosen — removes moral weight
Choice is so minor that the eventual consequences feel disproportionate
signals_in_text: Crossing a line the character previously respected
A moment of guilty awareness immediately suppressed

nodes.label: Apparent Success
nodes.definition: The fatal choice initially appears to work. The protagonist gains what they sought, reinforcing the belief that the transgression was justified.
entry_conditions: The fatal choice has been made
exit_conditions: Cracks begin to appear — the cost starts to surface
failure_modes: Success lasts too long — the story loses tension
No seeds of the coming unraveling are planted
signals_in_text: Small warning signs the character ignores

nodes.label: Unraveling
nodes.definition: The consequences of the fatal choice begin to multiply. Each attempt to maintain the illusion of success requires further transgression, creating a compounding spiral.
entry_conditions: New threats emerge from the original choice
exit_conditions: The character's position is untenable; exposure or catastrophe is imminent
typical_variants: Cover-up requires more lies or violence
Allies turn as they discover the truth
The original flaw deepens under pressure
failure_modes: Unraveling is caused entirely by external bad luck rather than the flaw
Character never has a moment of recognition during the descent

nodes.label: Recognition (Anagnorisis)
nodes.definition: The protagonist sees clearly — often for the first time — the full scope of what they have done and what they have become. This is the tragic insight.
entry_conditions: The unraveling has proceeded far enough that denial is impossible
exit_conditions: The character understands but cannot reverse the damage
typical_variants: A mirror moment — seeing themselves through another's eyes
Realizing the thing they sacrificed was the thing they truly valued
failure_modes: Recognition never comes — the character remains deluded (this can work but changes the story's meaning)
Recognition is too neat and intellectual, lacking emotional devastation

nodes.label: Downfall (Catastrophe)
nodes.definition: The protagonist suffers the full consequences — death, exile, ruin, or permanent loss. The punishment is proportional to the transgression but feels inevitable rather than arbitrary.
entry_conditions: Recognition has occurred (or the character is beyond recognition)
typical_variants: Death by the protagonist's own hand or the hand of those wronged
Exile and permanent separation from what they valued
failure_modes: Downfall feels random rather than caused by the flaw
Downfall is so extreme it seems sadistic rather than meaningful

nodes.label: Aftermath
nodes.definition: The world after the protagonist's fall. Order may be restored, but the cost is visible. The tragedy's meaning is crystallized in what remains.
exit_conditions: A new equilibrium is established, scarred by the events
typical_variants: A chorus or narrator reflects on the meaning
The world is diminished by the loss
failure_modes: Aftermath is skipped — the story ends at the moment of death
Aftermath is so tidy it undermines the tragedy's weight
signals_in_text: Restoration of order at a cost

edges.label: Flaw Activated
edges.preconditions: Elevated state is established; a situation arises that targets the specific flaw
edges.effects_on_stakes: What the character has to lose becomes clear as the temptation threatens to destabilize it
edges.effects_on_character: The latent flaw becomes active; internal conflict begins
edges.common_alternatives: Temptation comes from within (ambition swells) or without (someone offers a shortcut)
edges.anti_patterns: Flaw appears for the first time at this moment with no prior hints

edges.label: Crosses the Line
edges.preconditions: Temptation is active; the character deliberates and chooses
edges.effects_on_stakes: Irreversibility is established; the character cannot undo what is done
edges.effects_on_character: Moral identity shifts — the character is now someone who has done this
edges.common_alternatives: Choice is made in a moment of passion; choice is calculated and deliberate
edges.anti_patterns: Character is forced into the choice with no alternative — removes moral agency

edges.label: Reaps the Reward
edges.preconditions: Fatal choice is made; immediate consequences appear positive
edges.effects_on_stakes: The character now has more to lose, making the eventual fall steeper
edges.effects_on_character: Confidence grows; the flaw is reinforced by success
edges.common_alternatives: Success is immediate; success builds gradually as the plan works
edges.anti_patterns: Success is so complete the character has nothing left to want — tension disappears

edges.label: Cracks Appear
edges.preconditions: Apparent success is in place; the first consequence of the fatal choice surfaces
edges.effects_on_stakes: Each new problem requires a new transgression to contain, escalating the stakes
edges.effects_on_character: Paranoia, isolation, and deepening moral compromise
edges.common_alternatives: Cracks come from within (guilt) or without (discovery by others)
edges.anti_patterns: Unraveling is triggered by pure coincidence rather than consequences of the choice

edges.label: Sees the Truth
edges.preconditions: The unraveling has reached a point where denial is impossible
edges.effects_on_stakes: The character knows the end is coming; the question shifts to how they face it
edges.effects_on_character: Self-knowledge arrives too late to change the outcome
edges.common_alternatives: Recognition is forced by an external event; recognition emerges from internal reflection
edges.anti_patterns: Recognition is skipped — the character falls without ever understanding why

edges.label: Falls
edges.preconditions: Recognition has occurred; the consequences arrive in full
edges.effects_on_character: The character faces destruction with the clarity of recognition
edges.common_alternatives: Character accepts the fall with dignity; character rages against it; character attempts one final act
edges.anti_patterns: Downfall is caused by something unrelated to the flaw or the fatal choice

edges.label: World Endures
edges.preconditions: The protagonist's fate is sealed; the world must continue
edges.effects_on_stakes: Stakes are resolved but the cost is permanent
edges.effects_on_character: The protagonist's legacy — cautionary, mourned, or both — is established
edges.common_alternatives: World is better off but scarred; world is diminished by the loss; successor faces the same temptation
edges.anti_patterns: Aftermath pretends the tragedy never happened; restoration is too clean
